title: Simple Insertion with EcoRI
description: A GFP fragment arrives with EcoRI sites at both ends. Cut pUC19 at its EcoRI site in the MCS and ligate the insert in.
hints: EcoRI cuts G^AATTC, leaving a 4-base 5' AATT overhang. Because the same enzyme was used on both ends of the insert, the fragment can go in either direction.
Expect 2 recombinant orientations plus 1 vector self-ligation background — 3 products total, but only 2 distinct recombinant types.

vector.name: pUC19
vector.sequence_hint: MCS region contains a single EcoRI site at ~position 396.

insert.name: GFP fragment
insert.description: GFP coding sequence flanked by EcoRI sites on both ends (GAATTC...GFP...GAATTC).

teaching_point: When the same enzyme cuts both ends of an insert, the fragment can ligate in either orientation — giving 2 possible recombinant products plus the self-ligated (background) vector. Use blue-white selection or sequencing to identify the correct clone.

title: Directional Cloning with BamHI + HindIII
description: Clone a luciferase reporter directionally between BamHI and HindIII sites in the pUC19 MCS. The insert has a BamHI site on the left and a HindIII site on the right.
hints: BamHI leaves a GATC 5' overhang; HindIII leaves an AGCT 5' overhang. These two overhangs are NOT complementary to each other, so the insert can only go in one orientation.
Cut the vector first with BamHI (or HindIII) — you need to cut with both enzymes, but for this level start with BamHI.
Self-ligation of the vector is impossible when the two ends are incompatible, which is a key advantage of dual-enzyme directional cloning.

vector.name: pUC19
vector.sequence_hint: MCS has BamHI at ~421 and HindIII at ~447.

insert.name: Luciferase reporter
insert.description: Firefly luciferase coding sequence with BamHI (left end) and HindIII (right end).

teaching_point: Using two different restriction enzymes creates incompatible ends on the vector: BamHI (GATC) and HindIII (AGCT) cannot self-ligate and force the insert into a single orientation — the hallmark of directional cloning.

title: Blunt-End Cloning with EcoRV
description: A PCR product with blunt ends must be cloned into the pUC19 EcoRV site. Understand why blunt-end ligation is inefficient and why self-ligation is a major problem.
hints: EcoRV (GAT^ATC) generates blunt ends — no overhang to guide ligation.
Any blunt end can ligate with any other blunt end, so vector self-ligation is possible and common. The number of ligation products is higher than with sticky ends.
Blunt-end ligation requires more T4 ligase and longer reaction times. PEG or DMSO additives are often used.

vector.name: pUC19
vector.sequence_hint: MCS region contains an EcoRV site (GATATC).

insert.name: PCR product (blunt)
insert.description: A 500 bp PCR product with Klenow-polished blunt ends.

teaching_point: Blunt ends are compatible with all other blunt ends. This means the vector can self-ligate (background), the insert can circularise, and the insert can go in either orientation — giving up to 4 ligation products. Phosphatase treatment of the vector reduces self-ligation background.

title: Compatible but Site-Destroying Ends: SalI + XhoI
description: The vector is cut with SalI (GTCGAC, TCGA overhang) and the insert has XhoI ends (CTCGAG, also TCGA overhang). The ends are compatible, but ligation creates a hybrid sequence that is cut by neither enzyme.
hints: SalI (G^TCGAC) and XhoI (C^TCGAG) both leave the same 5' TCGA overhang, so they can ligate together.
After ligation, the junction sequence is GTCGAG — this is neither a SalI (GTCGAC) nor an XhoI (CTCGAG) site. The restriction site is permanently destroyed.
This trick is useful for multi-fragment cloning, but means you cannot excise the insert later with either enzyme.

vector.name: pUC19
vector.sequence_hint: MCS contains a SalI site at ~435. SalI and XhoI share a compatible TCGA overhang.

insert.name: Synthetic gene with XhoI ends
insert.description: A codon-optimised gene with XhoI (CTCGAG) sites on both ends.

teaching_point: SalI and XhoI generate the same 5'-TCGA overhang and can co-ligate. The resulting hybrid junction (GTCGAG) is not recognised by either enzyme — the site is 'destroyed'. This feature is intentionally used in multi-gene assembly but must be planned carefully.

title: Multi-Insert Prediction: Two Inserts, Compatible Ends
description: Two separate DNA fragments (Insert A: BamHI/XbaI ends; Insert B: BamHI/XbaI ends) are mixed with a BamHI+XbaI-cut vector. Predict all possible ligation products — it's more than you think!
hints: Each insert can go in independently, but inserts can also ligate to each other before joining the vector — creating concatamers.
When two inserts have identical ends, they can form dimers (A+B, B+A, A+A, B+B) which can then insert into the vector.
The correct single-insert products (forward orientation only, since ends are asymmetric) plus concatamers gives many possible colonies.
Focus on the single-insert, directional products — these are the ones you want. Screen by colony PCR.

vector.name: pUC19
vector.sequence_hint: MCS has BamHI at ~421 and XbaI at ~431. The vector is cut with both enzymes.

insert.name: Insert A (promoter fragment)
insert.description: Promoter fragment with BamHI (left) and XbaI (right) ends. A second insert (Insert B, 580 bp) with the same end types is also present in the ligation reaction.

teaching_point: In multi-insert ligation reactions, fragments can ligate to each other as well as to the vector. Asymmetric ends (BamHI + XbaI) ensure directional insertion but cannot prevent insert concatamerisation. Screen many colonies by PCR to find single-insert clones.
